result.markdown:
---
title: "Pokémon and Their Strengths by Type, Stats, and Generation"
subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project"
author: "Max Henderson"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/pokemon.jpg
  data-background-size: stretch
  data-background-opacity: "0.7"
  data-slide-number: none
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: ['data/customtheming.scss']
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---


::: {.cell}
<style type="text/css">
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=DM+Sans:wght@400;500;700&display=swap');
.reveal, .reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3, .reveal p, .reveal li {
  font-family: 'DM Sans', sans-serif;
}
</style>
:::






## Motivation & Data

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Why Pokémon?**

- Grew up with the franchise, wanted to test intuitions against real numbers
- Common assumptions: Fighting types hit harder, legendaries are always stronger
- Is any of that actually true?
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**The Dataset**

- 949 Pokémon species, 22 variables
- Source: TidyTuesday (Apr 2025) with PokeAPI
- Covers Generations 1–7
- Legendary/mythical flags joined from PokeAPI separately
:::
:::::

## Our Two Questions

1.  **Type, attack & weight** — Is there a relationship between a Pokémon's primary type, its attack stat, and its weight? Do types like Fighting or Dragon hit harder?

2.  **Legendary status & power creep** — Do legendary and mythical Pokémon consistently outperform standard species across every generation? Is there evidence that newer generations introduced stronger Pokémon?

## Q1 — Attack by Type


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/slide-q1-plot1-1.png){width=2100}
:::
:::


Fighting & Dragon sit highest, the reputation checks out. Fairy & Psychic sit lowest, yet both have high-attack outliers that can compete with the strongest of any type.

## Q1 — Does Weight Predict Attack?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/slide-q1-plot2-1.png){width=2100}
:::
:::


Every type trends positive, heavier generally means harder-hitting. Fairy, Ice & Flying are the strongest relationships. Psychic shows almost none, which fits its aesthetic.

## Q2 — Legendaries vs. Standard by Generation


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/slide-q2-plot1-1.png){width=2100}
:::
:::


Legendaries sit above standard Pokémon in all cases. The legendary boxes are also much tighter, suggesting the legendaries are designed around a fixed power budget (\~580–600 total stats).

## Q2 — Is There Power Creep?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/slide-q2-plot2-1.png){width=2100}
:::
:::


No meaningful power creep. Both lines stay fairly flat, standard Pokémon hover around 400, legendaries around 610, across all 7 generations. The Gen 7 legendary dip is a few Pokémon pulling the mean down.

## Conclusions

**Q1: Type, attack & weight**

- Type does predict attack: Fighting and Dragon lead, Fairy and Psychic trail
- Weight correlates with attack for most types, but strength varies, Fairy and Ice are surprisingly strong, Psychic shows almost no relationship

**Q2: Legendary status & power creep**

- Legendaries consistently outperform standard Pokémon in every generation
- No evidence of power creep, stat levels have stayed stable across years

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
